title: Hydrocarbons and Haloalkanes
subject: Chemistry
content:
# Hydrocarbons and Haloalkanes

## Quick Synopsis

Hydrocarbons introduce addition, substitution, elimination, and aromatic reactions. Haloalkanes connect structure to nucleophilic substitution and elimination mechanisms.

## Must Know

- Alkanes mainly undergo free-radical substitution.
- Alkenes and alkynes undergo electrophilic addition.
- Markovnikov and anti-Markovnikov additions depend on mechanism.
- Benzene undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution rather than addition under normal conditions.
- Haloalkanes undergo SN1, SN2, E1, and E2 depending on substrate, nucleophile/base, solvent, and temperature.
- Leaving group ability generally increases down halogen group.

## Formulas / Rules

- Hydrogen deficiency helps identify unsaturation and rings.
- Saytzeff product is more substituted alkene in many eliminations.
- Wurtz reaction couples alkyl halides with sodium in dry ether.
- Friedel-Crafts uses Lewis acid catalysts such as AlCl3.

## JEE Focus

- Know ozonolysis products to locate double bonds.
- Use peroxide effect only for HBr addition to alkene.
- SN2 favors primary substrate, strong nucleophile, polar aprotic solvent.
- SN1 favors stable carbocation and polar protic solvent.

## Common Traps

- Aryl halides resist SN1/SN2 due to partial double bond character and unstable phenyl cation.
- Peroxide effect does not apply to HCl or HI.
- Friedel-Crafts fails on strongly deactivated rings.
- E2 needs anti-periplanar beta hydrogen in many cases.

## 10-Minute Revision Checklist

- Can you predict alkene addition products?
- Can you identify aromatic substitution directing effects?
- Can you choose SN1 vs SN2 vs E1 vs E2?
- Can you solve ozonolysis questions?
